findings.evidence: <main> at line 27 contains another <main> at line 39. Only one <main> landmark is valid per document.

findings.description: Ensure the main landmark is at top level
findings.evidence:
Fix any of the following:
  The main landmark is contained in another landmark. [<main>]

findings.description: Ensure the document has at most one main landmark
findings.evidence:
Fix any of the following:
  Document has more than one main landmark [<main>]

Fix any of the following:
  The landmark must have a unique aria-label, aria-labelledby, or title to make landmarks distinguishable [<main>]

findings.description: The NC Water Resources / Environmental Quality logo appears in the PDF header but is not rendered as a visible image in the HTML version on page 1.
findings.evidence: PDF page 1 shows the NC logo with 'Water Resources ENVIRONMENTAL QUALITY' text beneath it in the upper left. The HTML page 1 also shows this logo, so checking more carefully - the HTML does include the logo image. No issue found on closer inspection.

findings.description: The memorandum header information (To, From, Subject, date, introductory paragraphs, and table of contents) appears twice in the HTML — once inside the <header> block and again inside the <main> block. In the PDF, this content appears only once on page 1.
findings.evidence: The HTML has a <header> section containing the full memo metadata (To: Secretary Donald R. van der Vaart, From: Andrew Pitner, Subject: Meeting Officer's Report..., the two introductory paragraphs, and the outline list I-IV), then immediately after, the <main> section repeats all of this same content with the h1 'MEMORANDUM' heading.

findings.description: The HTML adds internal navigation anchor links (TOC linking to section IDs) in the outline list that do not exist in the PDF. However, per instructions, internal anchor links added for accessibility are enhancements and not errors. No external URLs were fabricated.
findings.evidence: HTML contains History/Background etc. in the TOC nav. These are accessibility enhancements. Noting for completeness but this is NOT a true finding.
